title: Multiply fractions and whole numbers visually
description: Use area models, number lines, and tape diagrams to multiply a whole number times a fraction.
topic_path: Multiply fractions and whole numbers visually

assessment_items.question_content:
$4$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $4$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] square units
hints:
There are $4$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac45}$ of each square is shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $4 \times \blueE{\dfrac45}$ to find the total shaded area.
$\begin{align}
&\quad~~4 \times \blueE{\dfrac45}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4 \times \blueE4}{\blueE5}\\\\
&=\dfrac{16}{5}
\end{align}$

The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{16}{5}$ square units.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac15$ square units.

options.alt: A row of four identical rectangles. Each rectangle is divided into five equal parts. Each part of each rectangle is labeled one-fifth. The first four parts of each rectangle are shaded. The last part of each rectangle is not shaded.

assessment_items.question_content:
$4$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{5}{7}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ image 2]]

**What is $\dfrac{5}{7}$ of $4$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
There are $4$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac57}$ of each square is shaded.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $4 \times \blueE{\dfrac57}$ to find the total shaded area.
$\begin{align}
&\quad~~4 \times \blueE{\dfrac57}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4 \times \blueE5}{\blueE7}\\\\
&=\dfrac{20}{7}
\end{align}$
The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{20}{7}$ square units.

This can also be written as $2\dfrac67$ square units.

options.alt: A row of four identical rectangles. Each rectangle is divided into seven equal parts. Each part of each rectangle is labeled one-seventh. The first five parts of each rectangle are shaded. The last two parts of each rectangle are not shaded.

assessment_items.question_content:
$5$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of $5$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
There are $5$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac23}$ of each square is shaded.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $5 \times \blueE{\dfrac23}$ to find the total shaded area.

The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{10}{3}$ square units.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac13$ square units.

options.alt: A row of five identical rectangles. Each rectangle is divided into three equal parts. Each part of each rectangle is labeled one-third. The first two parts of each rectangle are shaded. The last part of each rectangle is not shaded.

assessment_items.question_content:
$6$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of $6$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
There are $6$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac35}$ of each square is shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $6 \times \blueE{\dfrac35}$ to find the total shaded area.

The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{18}{5}$ square units.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac35$ square units.

options.alt: Six identical squares. Each square is divided into five equal parts. Each part of each square is labeled one-fifth. The first three parts of each square are shaded. The last two parts of each square are not shaded.

assessment_items.question_content:
$6$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of their area shaded.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of $6$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
There are $6$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac23}$ of each square is shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $6 \times \blueE{\dfrac23}$ to find the total shaded area.

$\begin{align}
&\quad~~ 6 \times \blueE{\dfrac23}\\\\
&=\dfrac{6 \times \blueE2}{\blueE3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{12}{3}\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{12}{3}$ square units.

This can also be written as $4$ square units.

options.alt: Six identical squares. Each square is divided into three equal parts. Each part of each square is labeled one-third. The first two parts of each square are shaded. The last part of each square is not shaded.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sara wants to multiply $\dfrac14\times8$.  So, she puts $8$ triangles into $4$ equal-sized groups.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac14$ of $8$?**   [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: We see a set of $8$ triangles. The set is split into $4$ equal sections, or *fourths*.

To find $\dfrac14$ of $8$, we can shade $1$ fourth. How many triangles are shaded?



[[☃ image 1]]

$2$ triangles are shaded.
$\dfrac14$ of $8$ equals $2$.

options.alt: A row of eight identical triangles. Lines divide the triangles into 4 groups with 2 triangles each.

assessment_items.question_content:
Veronica wants to multiply $\dfrac23\times15$.  So, she puts $15$ triangles into $3$ equal-sized groups.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac23$ of $15?~~$**    [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: We see a set of $15$ triangles. The set is split into $3$ equal sections, or *thirds*.

To find $\dfrac23$ of $15$, we can shade $2$ thirds. How many triangles are shaded?

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{10}$ triangles are shaded.
$\dfrac23$ of $15$ equals $10$.

options.alt: Three rows of fifteen identical triangles. Lines divide the triangles into 3 groups with 5 triangles each.

assessment_items.question_content:
Logan wants to multiply $\dfrac34\times20$.  So, he puts $20$ triangles into $4$ equal-sized groups.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac34$ of $20$?**    [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: We see a set of $20$ triangles. The set is split into $4$ equal sections, or *fourths*.

To find $\dfrac34$ of $20$, we can shade $3$ fourths. How many triangles are shaded?

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{15}$ triangles are shaded.
$\dfrac34$ of $20$ equals $15$.

options.alt: Four rows of twenty identical triangles. Lines divide the triangles into 4 groups with 5 triangles each.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the following image to find each product.**


[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac13$ of $6=$    [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\dfrac23$ of $6=$    [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: We see a set of $6$ triangles. The set is split into $3$ equal sections, or *thirds*.

To find $\dfrac13$ of $6$, we can shade $1$ third. How many triangles are shaded?


[[☃ image 1]]

$2$ triangles are shaded.

To find $\dfrac23$ of $6$, we can shade $2$ thirds. How many triangles are shaded?


[[☃ image 1]]

$4$ triangles are shaded.

$\dfrac13$ of $6$ equals $2$.

$\dfrac23$ of $6$ equals $4$.

options.alt: A row of six identical triangles. Lines divide the triangles into 3 groups of 2 triangles each.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the following image to find each product.**

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac{1}{5}\times10=$    [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\dfrac{2}{5}\times10=$    [[☃ numeric-input 2]]

$\dfrac{3}{5}\times10=$    [[☃ numeric-input 3]]

$\dfrac{4}{5}\times10=$    [[☃ numeric-input 4]]
hints: We see a set of $10$ triangles. The set is split into $5$ equal sections, or *fifths*.

$\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $10$.

To find $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $1$ fifth. How many triangles are shaded?

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $2$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $2$ fifths.


[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $4$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $3$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $6$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $4$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $8$ triangles.

These are the products.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10 &= 2\\\\
\dfrac{2}{5} \times 10 &= 4\\\\
\dfrac{3}{5} \times 10 &= 6\\\\
\dfrac{4}{5} \times 10 &= 8
\end{align}$

options.alt: A row of ten identical triangles. Lines divide the triangles into 5 groups with 2 triangles each.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the following image to find each product.**

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac15$ of $15=$    [[☃ numeric-input 3]]

$\dfrac25$ of $15=$    [[☃ numeric-input 2]]

$\dfrac35$ of $15=$    [[☃ numeric-input 4]]

$\dfrac45$ of $15=$    [[☃ numeric-input 5]]
hints: We see a set of $15$ squares. The set is split into $5$ equal sections, or *fifths*.

$\dfrac{1}{5} \times 15$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $15$.

To find $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $15$, we can shade $1$ fifth. How many squares are shaded?


[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $3$ squares.

To find $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of $15$, we can shade $2$ fifths.


[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $6$ squares.

To find $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of $15$, we can shade $3$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $9$ squares.

To find $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $15$, we can shade $4$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $12$ squares.

$\dfrac15$ of $15$ equals $3$.

$\dfrac25$ of $15$ equals $6$.

$\dfrac35$ of $15$ equals $9$.

$\dfrac45$ of $15$ equals $12$.

options.alt: A row of fifteen identical squares. Lines divide the squares into 5 groups with 3 squares each.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the following image to multiply.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac16$ of $2?$** [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
To find $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of $2$, we can split $2$ wholes into $6$ equal pieces.

Each piece represents $\dfrac13$ of one whole:

[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can shade $1$ of the $6$ pieces to find $\dfrac16$ of $2$:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac13$ is shaded.
$\dfrac16$ of $2$ equals $\dfrac13$ .

options.alt: A row of two equal-sized rectangles. Each rectangle is split into 3 equal parts for a total of 6 parts, The first part is shaded and below the shaded part is a curved bracket labeled one-sixth of 2. Above both rectangles is a curved bracket labeled 2.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the following image to multiply.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac58$ of $4?$** [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
To find $\dfrac{5}{8}$ of $4$, we can split $4$ wholes into $8$ equal pieces.

Each piece represents $\dfrac12$ of one whole:



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can shade $5$ of the $8$ pieces to find $\dfrac{5}{8}$ of $4$:



[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac52$ is shaded.  


$\dfrac58$ of $4$ equals $\dfrac52$ .

options.alt: A row of four equal-sized rectangles. Each rectangle is split into 2 equal parts for a total of 8 parts, The first 5 parts are shaded and below the shaded parts is a curved bracket labeled five-eighths of 4. Above the row of rectangles is a curved bracket labeled 4.

**What is $\dfrac34$ of $2 ? $** [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:

Each piece represents $\dfrac12$ of one whole:

Now we can shade $3$ of the $4$ pieces to find $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $2$:

$\dfrac34$ of $2$ equals $\dfrac32$ .

options.alt: A row of two equal-sized rectangles. Each rectangle is split into 2 equal parts for a total of 4 parts, The first three parts are shaded and below the shaded parts is a curved bracket labeled three-fourths of 2. Above both rectangles is a curved bracket labeled 2.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the following image to multiply.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac12$ of $5?$** [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
To find $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $5$, we can split $5$ wholes into $2$ equal parts.

Each piece represents $\dfrac12$ of one whole:

[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can shade half of the pieces to find $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $5$:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac52$ is shaded.
$\dfrac12$ of $5$ equals $2\dfrac12$ .

options.alt: A row of five equal-sized rectangles. Each rectangle is split into 2 equal parts for a total of 10 parts, The first five parts are shaded and below the shaded parts is a curved bracket labeled one-half of 5. Above the row of  rectangles is a curved bracket labeled 5.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the following image to multiply.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac12$ of $7?$**   [[☃ input-number 2]]
hints:
To find $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $7$, we can split $7$ wholes into $2$ equal parts.

Each part represents $3\dfrac12$ :

[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can shade half of the pieces to find $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $7$:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac72$ is shaded.
$\dfrac12$ of $7$ equals $3\dfrac12$ .

options.alt: A row of seven equal-sized rectangles. Each rectangle is split into 2 equal parts for a total of 14 parts, The first seven parts are shaded and below the shaded parts is a curved bracket labeled one-half of 7. Above both rectangles is a curved bracket labeled 7.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the following image to multiply.**


[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac45 \times 10=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The big rectangle has a length of $10$. It is cut into $5$ equal pieces, or *fifths*.

Each fifth of $10$ has a length of $2$ on the number line:


[[☃ image 1]]

We can find $\dfrac45$ of $10$ on the number line by counting $4$ lengths of $2$:



[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac45\times10=8$

options.alt: A number line labeled 0 to 10 with tick marks every 1 unit. Above the number line is a tape diagram divided into 5 equal parts. 4 parts are shaded. The shaded parts of the tape diagram are between tick mark 0 and tick mark 8 of the number line. The unshaded parts of the tape diagram are between tick mark 8 and tick mark 10.

$\dfrac57\times7=$    [[☃ numeric-input 2]]

[[☃ image 1]]

We can see where $\dfrac57$ of $7$ is on the number line by counting $5$ lengths of $1$:


[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac57\times7=5$

options.alt: A number line labeled from 0 to 7 with tick marks every 1 unit. Above the number line is a tape diagram divided into 7 equal parts. 5 parts are shaded. The shaded parts of the tape diagram are between tick mark 0 and tick mark 5 of the number line. The unshaded parts of the tape diagram are between tick mark 5 and tick mark 7.
